text: The video shows a thick liver and a larger stomach following sleeve gastrectomy, with the fundus dissected and removed. The antrum of the stomach is retained at over four centimeters, and the body appears wider than in a single-stage sleeve procedure.

The surgeon moves the liver retractor to the right, lifting the right lobe of the liver and the falciform ligament to clear the surgical field. The duodenum is dissected using both blunt and sharp techniques and divided approximately four centimeters from the pylorus. The cecum is identified, and the ileocecal junction is located using the appendix as a marker. The terminal ileum is measured from this point, extending 205 to 300 centimeters.

A stray suture is inserted to fix the first part of the duodenum to the loop of ileum, aiding in positioning for bowel cutting and suturing. The suture lifts the anastomosis into view, facilitating the procedure. The surgeon uses a two-relock PDS suture for this step.

In the past I showed you how I use the Vicryl as the second layer. The first layer using the V-Log PDS takes away the tension of the anastomosis and the suture line gives a better purchase for the suture. Anastomosis itself may take about twenty minutes, therefore it's not a difficult one to do for anybody. In the past I used the anastomosis using the circular stapler but I wasn't happy with it and gave up after a couple of cases long time ago.

The surgeon opens the duodenum and ileum using a diatomic hook, followed by suturing with PDS to create a watertight suture line. The posterior layer is sutured first, and then the anterior layer, ensuring the lumen remains patent by carefully taking only one cut.

At the corner of the wound, the surgeon applies submucosal sutures, invaginating the wound slightly. After placing a couple of sutures, the technique shifts from backstitching to a normal suture method. The stay suture is lifted, providing tension to the suture line, allowing for quick and effortless completion. The suture extends beyond the opened ends of the ileum and duodenum, with locks applied to prevent slipping.

The surgeon continues the first posterior layer of sutures as the second anterior layer, ensuring the corner of the suture line is covered without defects. A 20 Robinson tube is inserted and kept in place for 24 hours. The excised portion of the greater curvature of the stomach is removed.
